coredata.dc:title: Electrochemical strategy for pyrophosphatase detection Based on the peroxidase-like activity of G-quadruplex-Cu2+ DNAzyme
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yonghong
Wu, Yaohui
Liu, Wen
Chu, Lei
Liao, Zhibin
Guo, Wentao
Liu, Gao-Qiang
He, Xiaoxiao
Wang, Kemin
coredata.dc:description: A new simple and highly sensitive electrochemical method for pyrophosphatase (PPase) activity detection was developed based on the peroxidase-like activity of G-quadruplex-Cu2+ DNAzyme. In the absence of PPase, Cu2+ could coordinate with pyrophosphate (PPi) to form Cu2+-PPi compound. While in the presence of PPase, it could destroy the coordinate compound because PPase catalyzed the hydrolysis of PPi into inorganic phosphate and produced free Cu2+, which then could be coupled with G-rich DNA to form G-quadruplex-Cu2+ DNAzyme. The formation of a mimic enzyme (G-quadruplex-Cu2+ DNAzyme) was immobilized on the surface of screen-printed gold electrode (SPGE). Using 3, 3′, 5, 5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) as a redox mediator and H2O2 as an enzyme substrate, the DNAzyme catalyzed the reduction of H2O2 to generate quantitative chronoamperometric signal. The catalytic activity of G-quadruplex-Cu2+ DNAzyme for TMB-H2O2 reaction was proportional to the activity of PPase, based on which, a simple and sensitive turn-on electrochemical method for PPase activity was thus developed for the first time. The chronoamperometric intensity of the system had a linear relationship with the PPase activities in the range of 1.0–50.0mU/mL and the detection limit could be down to 0.6mU/mL (S/N = 3). This proposed method was selective, cost-effective and convenient without any labels or complicated operations, which was furthermore applied to screen the inhibitor for PPase with high efficiency.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chronoamperometric detection
Peroxidase-like activity
Pyrophosphatase
G-Quadruplex
DNAzyme

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrochemical characterization of the biosensor fabrication
Evaluation on the catalytic activity of G-quadruplex-Cu2+ DNAzyme

Research Foundation of education Bureau of Hunan Province, China ( 16A227 ), and the Hunan Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China for Distinguished Young Scholars ( 2015JJ1025 )

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Faradaic impedance spectra obtained: (a) bare gold screen-printed electrode, (b) G-quadruplex modified gold screen-printed electrode and (c) MCH blocked G-quadruplex modified gold screen-printed electrode.
A) Absorption curves: (a) G-quadruplex in TMB-H2O2 system; (b) Cu2+ + G-quadruplex in TMB-H2O2 system; (c) with only Cu2+ in TMB-H2O2 system.
Chronoamperometric curves (a) with 100mU/mL PPase and (b) without PPase in the G-quadruplex-Cu2+ DNAzymes sensing system.

Signal responses of the sensing system under different conditions: (A) Effect of the concentration of Cu2+ on the chronoamperometric intensity in the sensing system; (B) Effect of PPi concentration on the chronoamperometric intensity in the sensing system; (C) Effect of incubation time; (D) Effect of pH of HEPES buffer on the chronoamperometric intensity in the sensing system. 50mU mL−1 PPase was used in the experiments.

(A) Chronoamperometric curves against different activities of PPase (from a to h: 1.0, 2.0, 5.0, 10.0, 20.0, 30.0, 40.0, 50.0, 80.0, 100.0mU/mL. (B) Corresponding plots of chronoamperometric values upon different activities of PPase. Inset: Calibration curve showing the dependence of the chronoamperometric values on the various activities of the PPase.
(A) Specificity of G-Quadruplex-Cu2+ electrochemical detection system against target PPase (50mUmL−1), AChE (50mUmL−1), BSA (50ngmL−1), lysozyme (50mgmL−1), GOx (50mUmL−1) and trypsin (50mUmL−1).
Inhibition of PPase activity (50mU/mL PPase used in this case) toward different concentrations NaF (10μM, 20μM, 50μM, 0.1mM, 0.5mM, 1.0mM) by using the G-Quadruplex-Cu2+ system.
Schematic illustration of the electrochemical strategy for pyrophosphatase activity detection.
Comparison of different methods for PPase activity sensing.

head.ce:title: Electrochemical strategy for pyrophosphatase detection Based on the peroxidase-like activity of G-quadruplex-Cu2+ DNAzyme

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hunan Provincial Key Laboratory for Forestry Biotechnology, College of Life Science and Technology, Central South University of Forestry and Technology, 410004 Changsha, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hunan Provincial Key Laboratory for Forestry Biotechnology, College of Life Science and Technology, Central South University of Forestry and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics, College of Biology, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan University, Changsha 410082, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics, College of Biology, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new simple and highly sensitive electrochemical method for pyrophosphatase (PPase) activity detection was developed based on the peroxidase-like activity of G-quadruplex-Cu2+ DNAzyme. In the absence of PPase, Cu2+ could coordinate with pyrophosphate (PPi) to form Cu2+-PPi compound. While in the presence of PPase, it could destroy the coordinate compound because PPase catalyzed the hydrolysis of PPi into inorganic phosphate and produced free Cu2+, which then could be coupled with G-rich DNA to form G-quadruplex-Cu2+ DNAzyme. The formation of a mimic enzyme (G-quadruplex-Cu2+ DNAzyme) was immobilized on the surface of screen-printed gold electrode (SPGE). Using 3, 3′, 5, 5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) as a redox mediator and H2O2 as an enzyme substrate, the DNAzyme catalyzed the reduction of H2O2 to generate quantitative chronoamperometric signal. The catalytic activity of G-quadruplex-Cu2+ DNAzyme for TMB-H2O2 reaction was proportional to the activity of PPase, based on which, a simple and sensitive turn-on electrochemical method for PPase activity was thus developed for the first time. The chronoamperometric intensity of the system had a linear relationship with the PPase activities in the range of 1.0–50.0mU/mL and the detection limit could be down to 0.6mU/mL (S/N = 3). This proposed method was selective, cost-effective and convenient without any labels or complicated operations, which was furthermore applied to screen the inhibitor for PPase with high efficiency.

ce:section.ce:para: It was known that the conversion of one-molecular pyrophosphate (PPi) could be specifically catalyzed by the ubiquitous inorganic pyrophosphatase (PPase, a kind of ubiquitous hydrolytic enzyme) to forming two orthophosphate ions (Pi). This hydrolysis reaction was a high-energy discharge reaction, which thus in a typical manner could be applied in certain energetically unfavorable biochemical reactions to drive/or complement these reactions [1,2]. More than that, PPase was demonstrated and reported to have intimate relativity with some biological reactions (e.g., lipid synthesis and degradation, DNA synthesis, phosphorus metabolism, calcium absorption, carbohydrate metabolism and bone formation) and clinical diseases (e.g., hyperthyroidism, lung adenocarcinomas and colorectal cancer) [3–5]. Thus undisputedly, PPase played an essential role in energy anabolism taking place in all living organisms whose absence or insufficiency in all probability would cause cell death [6]. In the past decades, therefore, with a strongly increasing demand in clinical states and medical applications, in the nano-molar to micro-molar activities range in particular, detection and quantification of PPase in biological samples stayed being an area of intensive interests [7–10].

In this regard, to cater expectations in future point-of-care testing, till now, various analytical techniques were developed for the detection of PPase and PPi including high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [11], fluorimetric method [12], colorimetric assay [13], electrochemiluminescent [14], electrochemistry [15], and capillary electrophoresis [16]. Recently, using globulin stabilized gold nanoclusters, Xu et al. reported a biosensor for fluorometric detection of pyrophosphatase activity [17]. Sun et al. designed a real-time fluorescent and colorimetric dual-readout assay for PPase activity detection based on Cu2+-triggered oxidation of o-Phenylenediamine [18]. Xu et al. developed a label-free fluorometric method for inorganic PPase activity according to Cu+-catalyzed alkyne-azide cycloaddition click reaction [19]. Lin et al. proposed an electrochemical method for PPase activity measurement in terms of enzymatic hydrolysate-induced release of copper ion [20]. In spite of undeniable and remarkable advances they made, these reported assays somehow were unfortunately restrained by the complicated, time-consuming and laborious operations, expensive precursors, and specialized equipment. Thus, there remained urgent and high requirements for technically simple and effective method for PPase activity assay. Here, to be noted, the electrochemical technique was extensively applied in various biosensors because it exhibited appealing superiorities of simple instrumentation, high sensitivity, miniaturization, and excellent compatibility to assay colored and viscous samples with which it accordingly was believed increasingly to be an irreplaceable technique for PPase detection and using various signal amplifications could further enhance the sensitivity of electrochemical biosensors to some extent.

For a decade, DNAzyme systems were employed in various scientific disciplines and acted as stable catalytic units for the development of biosensors and a frequently used DNAzyme in biosensors was peroxidase-like G-quadruplex DNAzyme system [21,22]. While G-quadruplex, with a four-stranded structure derived from a guanine (G)-rich DNA sequence, was formed by stacked Hoogsteen hydrogen-bonded G-tetrad as the basic structural motif [23,24], Hemin might bind through π−π stacking because G-quadruplex contained unique G-tetrad planes. G-quadruplex-hemin complexes were intensively applied to construct colorimetric, electrochemical, electroluminescent, or fluorescent sensing platform [25–28]. Besides, numerous ligands were demonstrated to be able to bind to G-quadruplexes by stacking onto the G-tetrads, endowing the ligands with enhanced properties, because of exploring unique characteristics of G-quadruplexes. For instance, the assembled G-tetrads were further stabilized by metal ion coordination (including K+, Zn2+, Pb2+) [29–33]. Recently, it was demonstrated that the human telomeric DNA and Cu2+ were capable of being used to catalyze the Friedel-Crafts reaction with excellent enantioselectivity [34,35]. Interestingly, a DNAzyme was proposed with peroxidase mimetic activity simply assembled by GpG-Cu2+ complexes [36–38]. Inspired by so, it was found that G-quadruplex-Cu2+ DNAzyme performed sound peroxidase property catalyzing the TMB and H2O2 reaction and producing a high chronoamperometric intensity.

In this study, the use of screen-printed gold electrode (SPGE) for electrochemical determination of PPase, according to the peroxidase-like activity of G-quadruplex-Cu2+ DNAzyme, was described for the first time. At first, in the presence of PPase, the Cu2+ could be released from the Cu2+-PPi complex due to the hydrolysis of PPi into Pi under the catalysis of PPase. Next, the released Cu2+ could be bound to the G-quadruplex and thus triggering the catalytic activity of G-quadruplex-Cu2+ DNAzyme. Then the activity of DNAzyme was directly related to the released Cu2+, and accordingly this principle could be used for monitoring of the PPase activity in the light of the change in the electrochemical current signals, based on which, thus a simple and sensitive turn-on electrochemical method for PPase were achieved. In this way, this study provided a new approach for quantitative and inexpensive PPase activity detection. Furthermore, this new type of sensor comprising a DNAzyme also could be a competitive tool for screening inhibitor. Moreover, the employment of the SPGE enabled the detection of enzyme activity in a disposable and no cumbersome operation and with reasonably low cost using small amounts of sample volumes. As a result, this demonstration was much likely to offer a totally new principle to fabrication of stable biosensors in a sensitivity and selectivity manner.

Hydrogen peroxide (30%) was acquired from Sinopharm chemical reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). 3, 3′, 5, 5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), PPase, trypsin, acetylcholinesterase (AChE), bovine serum albumin (BSA), lysozyme, glucose oxidase (GOx), 6-mercaptohexanol (MCH) and Tris (2-carboxyehtyl) phosphine hydrochloride (TCEP) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (China). All solutions were prepared and diluted using ultrapure water (18.2MΩcm) from the Millipore Milli-Q system (Millipore, Billerica, MA). Other reagents were of analytical grade and used as received without any further treatment steps.

The G-rich DNA sequence was commercially synthesized and purified by Shanghai Sangon Biological Engineering Technology & Services Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China), and was listed as follow. The biosensor was fabricated through self-assembly via Au-S bonds between the thiolated G-rich DNA and the clean screen-printed gold electrode.
G-rich DNA: 5′-GGG TTA GGG TTA GGG TTA GGGTTTT-3′-SH

The absorption spectra were recorded on a Nanodrop 2000 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc.). Electrochemical measurements were performed at room temperature using a CHI660E electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua Instrument Corporation, China). The measurements used a portable screen-printed electrode (DRP-C220AT, Metrohm China Limited), which consisted of a 4mm diameter gold working electrode, a Ag reference electrode and a gold counter electrode. The potentials were with respect to Ag reference electrode. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and chronoamperometry were used in sensor interrogation at room temperature. The potentials were with respect to Ag reference electrode. EIS measurements were carried out in the electrolyte solution of 10mM PBS buffer (pH 7.4) containing 5mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- and 0.1M KNO3 at room temperature. EIS was obtained by the biasing potential 0.21V, applying 10mV amplitude in the frequency range of 0.1Hz to 100kHz. Chronoamperometric measurements were performed at an applied potential of + 100mV vs the Ag pseudo-reference electrode for 200s. All the measurements were performed by independent experiments with repetition for at least five times, and the error bars were shown in the figures.

The SPGE was cleaned with ethanol and ultrapure water for 5min each at first. Briefly, 200μLG-rich DNA, denatured in HEPES buffer (pH 7.4), was heated to 90°C for 5min and then cooled down slowly to room temperature for more than 1h in order to allow the probes to fold into their proper conformations (G-quadruplex). Next, 50μL of 200μMG-quadruplex solution was mixed with 50μL of 10mM TCEP and left at room temperature in dark for 1h to reduce the disulphide bonds. This solution was then diluted with HEPES buffer to reach a probe concentration of 10µM. 20μL diluted G-quadruplex solution was then dispensed on the clean gold surface for 2h at room temperature to be self-assembled on SPGE. Then the G-quadruplex modified SPGE was treated with 1mM MCH solution for 30min to block the remaining bare region and thoroughly rinsed with HEPES buffer to remove nonspecifically absorbed G-quadruplex.

Firstly, 100μL PPi (50μM) and 100μL Cu2+ (20μM) were mixed to form PPi-Cu2+ complexes. Next, a volume of 10μL of PPase at various activities and 90μL of PPi-Cu2+ complex were mixed in 100μL HEPS buffer (50mM, pH 7.4) to obtain the released Cu2+. Then, the mixture reaction solution was centrifuged for 10min, and 20μL of the supernatant was deposited onto the G-quadruplex modified SPGE electrode and incubated for 90min. After washing with 50mM HEPS buffer (pH 7.4), 20μL of the TMB-H2O2 solution containing 1mM TMB as an indicator and 20mM H2O2 were added on the sensor surface. After stabilization of the background current, the chronoamperometric measurements were carried out at an applied potential of + 100mV vs the Ag pseudo-reference electrode. The analytical signal was the current measured 200s after the addition of the TMB-H2O2 substrate.

By using sodium fluoride (NaF), as a model inhibitor, the influence of inhibitor on PPase activity had also been quantitatively analyzed to demonstrate the potential application of this developed assay. A typical inhibitor assay mixture contained 50.0mU/mL PPase, buffer and various concentrations of NaF. After a brief mixing, these NaF-treated PPase solutions were added into the mixture reaction solution. The processes were similar to the assay of PPase activity.

Scheme 1 describes the electrochemical strategy for the detection of PPase activity based on the peroxidase-like activity of G-quadruplex-Cu2+ DNAzyme. To be specific, Cu2+ ions were added to PPi solution in order to form the coordinate compound PPi-Cu2+. The formed complex was incubated with PPase, resulting in the hydrolysis of PPi into orthophosphate, which does not complex with Cu2+. As a result, Cu2+ ions were released from the PPi-Cu2+ complex into solution. Next, to form an antiparallel G-quadruplex structure, the -SH modified G-rich DNA strand was denatured in HEPES buffer followed by being modified on the SPGE. Then, the G-quadruplex-Cu2+ DNAzyme complex was formed because of the release of Cu2+, which could catalyze the reduction of hydrogen peroxide in the presence of the TMB cosubstrate to produce quantitative electrochemical current signals. The chronoamperometric change of the sensing system was related to the activities of the added PPase. Besides, it was demonstrated that chronoamperometry technique is considered as a simple and highly sensitive signal transduction approach for the detection of different biomolecules. This G-quadruplex-Cu2+ DNAzyme system-based biosensor additionally explored a simple, sensitive, label-free route for the detection of PPase. To the best of our knowledge, however, no study was proposed on the PPase activity electrochemical detection by using G-quadruplex-Cu2+ DNAzyme. Accordingly, this demonstration was in all probability to perform an entirely novel means to fabrication of stable biosensors with sound sensitivity while requiring no direct oxidation of PPase.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical characterization of the biosensor fabrication
ce:section.ce:para: EIS measurements, known as an efficient method for investigation on the interface properties of modified electrode, were used to determine the characteristics of the sensor fabrication and interrogation including several steps and details. Fig. 1 showed the EIS performance of the electroactive ion pair [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− on the surface of the SPGE modification. In terms of bare gold screen-printed electrode, a straight line was obtained at low-frequency with a small semicircle at a high-frequency region (curve a). It demonstrated that the process was essentially diffusion-controlled for the redox couple, indicating an extremely low electron transfer resistance. As G-quadruplex probes were modified onto the electrode surface through the thiol groups, a semicircular portion was performed in the higher frequency region, suggesting a large electron-transfer resistance rising in Ret (curve b). This finding thus manifested that the modification of the thiol groups on the SPGE surface obstructed interfacial electron transfer between the electrode and the electroactive indicators because of weak conductivity of DNA. Also it demonstrated that the G-quadruplex probes had been immobilized on the SPGE surface. While when MCH, as blocking agent, was self-assembled to the G-quadruplex modified SPGE, the EIS Nyquist plots exhibited an increasing electrochemical impedance response in [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− (curve c), stating that the electrode surface blocked electron transfer by MCH and offered a useful barrier in solution against electron transfer of the redox species. In this case according to design strategy, these EIS results indicated that the modification process of the electrode was fabricated successfully.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation on the catalytic activity of G-quadruplex-Cu2+ DNAzyme
ce:section.ce:para: In order to examine the peroxidase-like catalytic activity of G-quadruplex-Cu2+ DNAzyme, Cu2+ was mixed with G-quadruplex to catalyze the reaction of TMB-H2O2 system. Known from the absorption spectra ( Fig. 2), the free G-quadruplex showed almost no light absorption because the G-quadruplex could not catalyze the TMB-H2O2 system in the absence of Cu2+ (curve a). While after it intercalated with Cu2+, the absorption intensity of it increased dramatically (curve b). This phenomenon was due to the high catalytic activity of G-quadruplex-Cu2+ DNAzyme. In curve c, however, a slight increase of the absorption intensity was observed when Cu2+ existed alone because Cu2+ also had a certain ability to catalyze the TMB-H2O2 system. Nevertheless, the interfacial reaction, the Cu2+ would be washed away, affecting negligibly the sensing system. As a result, in regard to the system of catalyzing TMB-H2O2 by G-quadruplex-Cu2+ DNAzyme, the concentration of Cu2+ here was particularly important, and the G-quadruplex-Cu2+ DNAzymes were proved to have the peroxidase-like catalytic activity.

Served as an electron shuttle and being capable of diffusing into and out of the redox site of the DNAzyme here, TMB could be oxidized on the electrode surface and yield the electrocatalytic currents observed. To be detailed, chronoamperometry provided a direct way to characterize the DNAzyme-catalyzed electrochemical process. In order to investigate the feasibility of G-quadruplex-Cu2+ DNAzyme as the probes for the target detection, the electrochemical behavior of modified electrode was performed by using chronoamperometry. And it was observed with a decay curve for the i-t relationship on the onset of the potential, reaching a steady-state current within ∼ 200s ( Fig. 3). It was shown that the chronoamperometric current for 100.0mU/mL of PPase (curve a) was ∼ 8 times stronger than that in the absence of PPase (curve b), demonstrating consequently the feasibility of this electrochemical assay in detection of the PPase activity.

In this study, Cu2+ played a vital role as a parameter. Provided the concentration of Cu2+ was at high level, an increasing number of PPi would be needed to bind with Cu2+, thus not in favor of achieving a low detection limit. While if it was too low, then the detection of PPase with high activity would possibly not be reached, leading to a confined detection range and poor sensitivity of the proposed biosensor. As a whole, hence through adding different concentrations of Cu2+ into G-quadruplex, the optimal concentration of Cu2+ was explored with a fixed concentration ultimately. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the signal intensity strengthened with increasing concentration of Cu2+ and reached a plateau when the concentration of Cu2+ exceeded 20.0μM. Accordingly, 20.0μM was fixed as optimized Cu2+ concentration in the experiments.

Adding PPi with different concentrations into the mixture containing 20.0μM Cu2+, the effect of PPi here on the sensing system was furthermore detected. In Fig. 4 B, the chronoamperometric intensity of the mixture increased significantly with further addition of PPi, performing a peak relationship with PPi concentration from 5.0 to 60μM. The signal exhibited maximum value when PPi concentration was 45μM, which thus would be beneficial to the subsequent quantitative PPase activity assay. Therefore, the optimized concentration of PPi was chosen at 45μM.

Also, reaction time was also studied as an effect parameter between PPi and PPase. As shown in Fig. 4 C, during the PPase-catalyzed hydrolysis process, along with the increasing hydrolytic time, the chronoamperometric intensity increased at the beginning and gradually reached a plateau after 30min. In order to save time, 30min, including competitive and hydrolytic reactions time, was chosen as the optimized time for the whole reaction process.

Besides, since HEPES buffer was essential in maintaining the bioactivity of PPase, thus the effect of the pH values of HEPES buffer needed to be explored as another important factor. As seen in Fig. 4 D, an obvious chronoamperometric intensity was acquired at pH 7.4 HEPES buffer solution, indicating a robust bioactivity of PPase. Unlike this, a higher or lower pH would cause lower chronoamperometric intensity as detected. Therefore, throughout this work HEPES buffer at pH 7.4 was used for the determination of PPase activity.

As proposed, the electrochemical method was adopted to assay PPase activity under the optimum conditions and the detection performance was executed through exposing the sensor to series of PPase activities. Relevant to different activities of the target in the testing buffers, the chronoamperometric responses of the sensor functioned as the basic value for target determination. Meanwhile, the electrochemical measurements were carried out in the electrolyte of HEPES buffer. In the absence of the PPase, a comparatively weak background chronoamperometric response was shown. Yet when the PPase activities increased, as shown in Fig. 5 A, the chronoamperometric response got stronger correspondingly when the amount of released Cu2+ combining with G-quadruplex. The relative chronoamperometric intensity for the standard assay solution increased with different activities of PPase ranging from 1.0 to 100.0mU/mL (Fig. 5 B). The results showed that the linear correlation was in the range of 1.0–50.0mU/mL (inset of Fig. 5 B), and the regression equation was A = 2.6611X + 45.905 (A is the chronoamperometric intensity, X is the activity of PPase), with the correlation R2 = 0.9632. In accordance with the signal amounting to 3 times the standard deviation of the blanks, the detection limit was 0.6mU/mL calculated, adequate for many applications. Compared with previous reports about colorimetric and fluorescent assay for PPase activity detection ( Table 1), the developed strategy presented superiorities of high sensitivity, low cost, and simple operation. Apart from sensor sensitivity, sensor properties of great importance here were also systematically evaluated such as reproducibility and selectivity. In detail, the relative standard deviation (RSD, n = 6) of the PPase activity detection was 7.83% with different electrodes from the same batch at 50mU/mL level, confirming the robust reproducibility of the sensor.

Aiming at investigating the specificity of such bioassay toward PPase by the proposed biosensor, several representative proteins/enzymes including AChE, BSA, lysozyme, GOx and trypsin were performed as control. In Fig. 6 , the results demonstrated persuasively that not even a single of above control samples could cause distinct chronoamperometric response in the bioassay system and impact on the detection of PPase activity. In the presence of interferents, the chronoamperometric intensities were very week while the signal of target had a high value in the presence of PPase. That attributed to the reason that these species were unable to destroy the complexation between Cu2+ and PPi, causing the G-quadruplex-Cu2+ DNAzyme not sequent forming, indicating thus that interference from others could be ignored and the proposed strategy showed excellent selectivity for PPase detection. Overall, these results demonstrated that the proposed method was selective in distinguishing other proteins/enzymes.

In a general way, NaF was treated typically as an effective inhibitor for PPase due to the formation of the [PPase-F-] intermediate, accordingly leading to enzymatic activity sharply decreasing. Herein, by using NaF as a model PPase inhibitor, the G-Quadruplex-Cu2+-based PPase activity bioassay system proposed could be extended to evaluate PPase inhibition. Along with the NaF concentrations in the analytical system increasing from 10μM to 1.0mM, the PPase activity would be inhibited and chronoamperometric intensity correspondingly decreased ( Fig. 7). Afterwards, a typical sigmoidal profile was observed when plotting the signal intensity values versus the NaF concentration, and the IC50 (the inhibitor concentration causing 50% inhibition of the enzyme activity) was calculated to be approximately 0.1mM under this experimental conditions. Thus it indicated that NaF could not cause the dissociation of Cu2+-PPi complex because the electrochemical signal in the presence of NaF was nearly the same as the background signal. As a consequence, above results demonstrated that NaF could be employed to evaluate the inhibitor efficiency of PPase activity, and the sensing system proposed in this work could be used in screening potential PPase inhibitors.

In summary, by making use of the peroxidase-like property of G-quadruplex-Cu2+ DNAzyme, an electrochemical sensor for sensitive detection of PPase activity was successfully designed and fabricated. Here, the G-quadruplex-Cu2+ DNAzyme system exhibited high catalytic ability on catalyzed reaction of TMB/H2O2, which was defined as a signal in quantifying the activity of PPase. On the basis of the competition reaction among PPi, G-quadruplex with Cu2+, a highly sensitive and selective electrochemical assay for PPase detection, was fulfilled owing to the adoption of screen of inhibitors for PPase. The biosensor performed the linear response ranging from 1.0 to 50.0mU/mL and the detection limit could reach as low as 0.6mU/mL. Besides, the cost of the detection was relatively low since the developed assay used SPGE and small amounts of sample volumes. Admittedly, compared with conventional techniques, this electrochemical strategy not only required no fluorescent label but offered a sensitive, low cost and efficient platform for quantitatively determining PPase activity, thus representing a user-friendly protocol for quantitative enzyme activity in further application and showing a promise as a powerful technique to be applied in clinical analysis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported in part by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21305164 and 31772374). Research Foundation of education Bureau of Hunan Province, China (16A227), and the Hunan Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China for Distinguished Young Scholars (2015JJ1025)
